59/100Foundations
B
Project Structure80/100 · weight 6% · 24ms · 2 issues
high
WhatChecks for standard project files: package.json, tsconfig.json, LICENSE, README, .gitignore, lockfile. Verifies test-to-source file ratio and that essential scripts (test, build) exist.
RiskMissing config files cause build failures in CI. Missing LICENSE makes the project legally ambiguous. No lockfile means non-reproducible builds — a dependency update can break production silently.
FixEnsure every project has package.json, tsconfig.json, LICENSE, .gitignore, and a lockfile. Add 'test' and 'build' scripts. Aim for at least one test file per source file.
foundpackage.json, tsconfig.json, .gitignore, README.md, lockfile
missingLICENSE
srcFiles90
testFiles5
testRatio6%
General
EMissing LICENSE — Open source licensemissing-file
WLow test-to-source ratio: 5 tests for 90 source files (6%)low-test-ratio
A
Lint100/100 · weight 5% · 5950ms · 0 issues
high
WhatRuns the project's linter (Biome or ESLint, auto-detected) and counts errors and warnings. Lint rules catch bugs, enforce consistency, and prevent common mistakes before they reach production.
RiskUnlinted code accumulates inconsistencies and latent bugs. Studies show that projects with active linting have 15-20% fewer production defects (Microsoft Research, 2019).
FixFix all lint errors. Warnings can be addressed incrementally. If no linter is configured, add Biome (@biomejs/biome) — it's the fastest linter for TypeScript with zero config needed.
errors0
warnings0
lintereslint

No issues found.

A
Type Check100/100 · weight 6% · 5220ms · 0 issues
critical
WhatRuns tsc --noEmit to find TypeScript compilation errors. Type errors mean the code may crash at runtime in ways the compiler could have prevented.
RiskType errors are bugs. Every unresolved type error is a potential runtime crash. TypeScript's type system exists to prevent entire categories of bugs — ignoring it negates its value.
FixFix all type errors. If you're migrating from JavaScript, enable strict mode gradually — start with 'strict: true' and fix errors file by file.
errors0
oktrue

No issues found.

F
Type Safety16/100 · weight 3% · 55ms · 70 issues
medium
WhatCounts unsafe type patterns: 'as any' casts, explicit ': any' annotations, @ts-ignore directives, @ts-nocheck, and non-null assertions (!.). Each weakens the type system's protection.
Risk'as any' silences the type checker at that point — any bug the types would have caught now slips through. @ts-ignore and @ts-nocheck disable type checking entirely for a line or file. Accumulated 'any' usage correlates with higher defect density.
FixReplace 'as any' with proper types or type guards. Use 'unknown' instead of 'any' when the type is genuinely unknown. Remove @ts-ignore comments by fixing the underlying type issue.
: any51
non-null assertion (!.)3
as any16

F
Code Standards0/100 · weight 3% · 59ms · 49 issues
medium
WhatChecks coding conventions: file naming (PascalCase for components, kebab-case for modules), file size limits (>300 lines flagged), code smells (console.log, var, ==, eval, innerHTML, TODO/FIXME), config hygiene (strict mode), and framework best practices (Tailwind vs inline styles).
RiskLarge files are hard to review and test. console.log in production leaks internal data. var causes hoisting bugs. == causes type coercion surprises. eval/innerHTML are security vulnerabilities. Inconsistent naming makes the codebase harder to navigate.
FixSplit files over 300 lines. Replace console.log with a proper logger or remove it. Use const/let, ===, and safe DOM APIs. Enable TypeScript strict mode.
filesScanned90
codeSmells0
largeFiles29
namingViolations0
